mappings.rationaleAI: Directly examines the division of Earth's 4.5-billion-year history into Eons, Eras, Periods, and Epochs — the Hadean, Archaean, Proterozoic, and Phanerozoic — and the geochronological and stratigraphic methods used to establish these divisions.

Britain's 600-million-year geological record spans Precambrian Scottish Highlands, Carboniferous coal swamps, Cretaceous chalk, and Pleistocene ice ages, providing a concrete stratigraphic case study across all major eras and periods of geologic time.

The Ediacara Biota are Precambrian life forms vanishing approximately 542 million years ago, representing the most significant known biological assemblage of the late Precambrian and key evidence about life before the Cambrian explosion.

Archaea separated from bacteria billions of years ago in deep Precambrian time and later combined to form eukaryotic cells, representing a fundamental evolutionary divergence of the earliest eon of Earth's history.

Life originated nearly four billion years ago during the Precambrian, and competing hypotheses — primordial soup, hydrothermal vents, RNA world — address the earliest biological events in Earth's geologic timeline.

Directly covers the Cambrian explosion approximately 550 million years ago, including rapid diversification of complex multicellular life, the Burgess Shale fossil record, and the environmental conditions of ocean chemistry and atmospheric oxygen that drove Cambrian speciation.

Fungi facilitated plant colonization of land approximately 450 million years ago during the Ordovician-Silurian transition, a pivotal terrestrialization event of the early Paleozoic that reshaped ecosystems and provides coverage for these otherwise underrepresented periods.

Covers the late Devonian transition approximately 400 million years ago when lobe-finned fish evolved limbs, lungs, and neck structures to become the first tetrapods, a defining biological event of the Devonian Period.

Respiratory lung evolution beginning approximately 400 million years ago during the Devonian Period, when early fish in shallow pools first developed air-breathing capacity, marks a key physiological innovation of the Devonian water-to-land transition.

Directly addresses the late Devonian mass extinction approximately 370 million years ago that eliminated roughly 70 percent of species, linking terrestrial forest development and soil formation to marine anoxia that devastated reef-building organisms and armored fish.

The most catastrophic mass extinction in Earth's history at approximately 250 million years ago spans the Permian-Triassic boundary — the transition from the Paleozoic to the Mesozoic Era — with causes including Siberian Traps volcanism and ocean anoxia.

The Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary at approximately 66 million years ago marks the end of the Mesozoic Era, with the Chicxulub impact, iridium anomaly evidence, and Deccan Traps volcanism debate defining the transition into the Cenozoic.

The catastrophism-versus-uniformitarianism debate centers on the 1970s revival of catastrophist thinking through evidence of a massive asteroid impact 65 million years ago at the end of the Cretaceous Period that caused dinosaur extinction.

A major Tertiary Period climate event approximately 56 million years ago when global temperatures rose over five degrees Celsius, destabilizing methane hydrates and causing widespread extinctions that reshaped early Cenozoic ecosystems.

The rise and diversification of mammals during the Cenozoic Era following the KT extinction 65 million years ago, with continental drift from Pangaea breakup creating isolated landmasses that enabled the distinct mammalian radiations characterizing the Tertiary Period.

Whale evolution from small land mammals to fully aquatic creatures over millions of years during the Tertiary Period, with fossil intermediates documenting one of the Cenozoic's most dramatic vertebrate transformations.

Covers ice ages throughout geological history including Precambrian Snowball Earth episodes and the Quaternary glaciation beginning up to 40 million years ago, with detailed treatment of Pleistocene glacial-interglacial cycles driven by Milankovitch orbital cycles and atmospheric CO2 levels.

Spans over six million years of hominin evolution during the late Tertiary and Quaternary Periods, with climate change driving speciation and extinction across the Pliocene and Pleistocene, directly documenting the biological events of Earth's most recent geologic periods.

Neanderthals inhabited Europe and Central Asia from approximately 400,000 to 28,000 years ago during the Pleistocene Epoch, and their adaptation, tool use, and eventual extinction or hybridization with Homo sapiens characterize a defining chapter of Quaternary prehistory.
